news_summary: Astronomers and private companies are making exciting advances by uncovering cosmic collisions, preparing for asteroid landings, and observing remarkable asteroid behavior near Earth.
news_summary_fr: Les astronomes et les entreprises privées réalisent des avancées passionnantes en mettant au jour des collisions cosmiques, en préparant des atterrissages sur des astéroïdes et en observant le comportement remarquable de ces derniers à proximité de la Terre.
news_summary_es: Los astrónomos y las empresas privadas están logrando avances fascinantes al descubrir colisiones cósmicas, prepararse para aterrizajes en asteroides y observar el extraordinario comportamiento de los asteroides cercanos a la Tierra.

articles.title: Astronomers discover nearby galaxy was shattered by cosmic crash
articles.summary: A nearby galaxy is behaving strangely—and now scientists know why. The Small Magellanic Cloud’s stars move in chaotic patterns because it slammed into its larger neighbor millions of years ago. That collision disrupted its structure and even created the illusion that its gas was rotating. The discovery means this once “textbook” galaxy may not be as typical as astronomers believed.
articles.content:
The Small Magellanic Cloud (SMC) is one of the Milky Way's closest galactic neighbors, a compact, gas-rich galaxy that can be seen with the naked eye from the southern hemisphere. It orbits our galaxy along with its larger companion, the Large Magellanic Cloud (LMC), and all three have been interacting for hundreds of millions of years. Because of its proximity, the SMC has been studied in detail for decades, with astronomers mapping its stars, tracking its gas, and measuring its motion.

Even so, one fundamental puzzle remained unresolved. Unlike most galaxies, the stars in the SMC do not move in orderly orbits around the center, leaving scientists searching for an explanation.

Collision Explains Missing Stellar Rotation

New research published in The Astrophysical Journal points to a dramatic answer. A team from the University of Arizona found that the SMC's unusual behavior is the result of a direct collision with the Large Magellanic Cloud. This finding also raises concerns about using the SMC as a standard example for understanding how galaxies formed and evolved over cosmic time.

"We are seeing a galaxy transforming in live action," said Himansh Rathore, a graduate student at Steward Observatory and the lead author of the paper. "The SMC gives us a unique, front-row view of something very transformative of a process that is critical to how galaxies evolve."

Gas, Gravity, and Disrupted Motion

The SMC contains more mass in gas than in stars. Under normal conditions, gas cools and settles into a rotating disk under gravity, much like the process that formed the flat, spinning plane of our solar system. However, earlier measurements using the Hubble Space Telescope and the European Space Agency's Gaia satellite showed that the SMC's stars were not following this expected pattern.

According to Rathore, the likely cause is a collision that occurred a few hundred million years ago. During this event, the SMC passed directly through the disk of the LMC. The gravitational forces involved disrupted the SMC's structure and scattered its stars into disorganized motion. At the same time, the dense gas in the LMC exerted strong pressure on the SMC's gas, effectively stripping away its rotation.

"Imagine sprinkling water droplets on your hand and moving it through the air -- as the air rushes past, the droplets get blown off because of the pressure it exerts. Something similar happened to the SMC's gas as it punched through the LMC," Rathore said.

Solving a Decades-Old Illusion

The study also resolves a long-standing contradiction about the SMC's gas. For years, observations suggested that the gas inside the galaxy was rotating. Since stars form from gas and typically inherit its motion, astronomers expected the stars to rotate as well. But that was not what they observed.

The new analysis shows that this apparent rotation was misleading. The collision stretched the SMC, and gas moving toward and away from Earth along this stretched shape can appear to rotate when viewed from certain angles.

Rethinking a Cosmic Benchmark

For decades, the SMC has served as an important reference point for studying how galaxies form stars and evolve. These new findings challenge that role.

"The SMC went through a catastrophic crash that injected a lot of energy into the system. It is not a 'normal' galaxy by any means," Besla said.

To reach these conclusions, the researchers used detailed computer simulations that matched the known properties of both galaxies, including their gas content, stellar mass, and positions relative to the Milky Way. They combined these models with theoretical calculations to understand how the SMC's gas behaved as it moved through the LMC's dense environment. The team also developed new techniques to interpret the scrambled motions of stars in a galaxy that has experienced a collision.

This matters because the SMC's small size, high gas content, and low abundance of heavy elements made it a key comparison for galaxies in the early universe. If it is still recovering from a major collision, it may no longer serve as a reliable model.

Clues About Dark Matter From a Galactic Impact

The collision may also provide new insight into dark matter. In a separate study published in 2025, the same team found that the impact left a visible mark on the Large Magellanic Cloud. Its central bar-shaped structure is tilted out of the galaxy's plane, a feature linked to the collision.

Rathore explained that the degree of this tilt depends on how much dark matter the SMC contains. This relationship offers a new way to estimate dark matter, which cannot be observed directly and is only detected through its gravitational influence.

"We are used to thinking of astronomy as a snapshot in time," Rathore said. "But these two galaxies have come very close together, gone right through one another, and transformed into something different."
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant scientific discovery about the Small Magellanic Cloud's collision with the Large Magellanic Cloud, which changes our understanding of galaxy evolution and offers new methods to study dark matter. This has broad implications for astrophysics and cosmology, providing meaningful insight into galactic dynamics and dark matter estimation, thus inspiring curiosity and expanding human knowledge.
articles.personality_title: Astronomers reveal galaxy’s strange behavior caused by cosmic collision
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – The Small Magellanic Cloud (SMC) is a small galaxy near our Milky Way. For years, scientists noticed its stars moved in a confusing way, unlike most galaxies where stars spin smoothly around the center. This strange motion puzzled astronomers.

**What happened** – A new study found that the SMC crashed into its bigger neighbor, the Large Magellanic Cloud (LMC), a few hundred million years ago. This cosmic crash shook up the SMC’s stars and gas, making their movements look chaotic instead of orderly. The collision even created an illusion that the gas was spinning when it really wasn’t.

**Impact** – This discovery changes how scientists see the SMC. It was once thought to be a normal example of a small galaxy, but now it’s clear the galaxy is still recovering from a huge crash. This means the SMC might not be a good model for understanding how other galaxies form and change. The research also offers a new way to study dark matter, a mysterious substance in space, by looking at how the crash tilted parts of the larger galaxy.

**What's next step** – Researchers will use these findings to improve their models of galaxy formation and evolution. They will also study how collisions like this affect dark matter, helping to learn more about this invisible material that shapes galaxies.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Scientists discovered that a past collision between two nearby galaxies explains the Small Magellanic Cloud’s unusual star movements and offers new clues about dark matter.
articles.personality_title_fr: Les astronomes découvrent qu’une collision cosmique a bouleversé une galaxie voisine
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Le Petit Nuage de Magellan (PNM) est une petite galaxie proche de notre Voie lactée. Pendant des années, les scientifiques ont remarqué que ses étoiles se déplaçaient de façon désordonnée, contrairement à la plupart des galaxies où les étoiles tournent régulièrement autour du centre. Ce mouvement étrange a intrigué les astronomes.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Une nouvelle étude a révélé que le PNM a percuté son voisin plus grand, le Grand Nuage de Magellan (GNM), il y a plusieurs centaines de millions d’années. Cette collision cosmique a bouleversé les étoiles et le gaz du PNM, rendant leurs mouvements chaotiques au lieu d’ordonnés. La collision a même créé une illusion que le gaz tournait alors que ce n’était pas le cas.

**Impact** – Cette découverte change la vision que les scientifiques avaient du PNM. On pensait autrefois que c’était un exemple normal de petite galaxie, mais maintenant on sait qu’elle est encore en train de se remettre d’un énorme choc. Cela signifie que le PNM pourrait ne pas être un bon modèle pour comprendre la formation et l’évolution des autres galaxies. La recherche offre aussi une nouvelle méthode pour étudier la matière noire, une substance mystérieuse dans l’espace, en observant comment la collision a incliné des parties de la galaxie plus grande.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs utiliseront ces résultats pour améliorer leurs modèles de formation et d’évolution des galaxies. Ils étudieront aussi comment les collisions affectent la matière noire, ce qui aidera à mieux comprendre cette matière invisible qui façonne les galaxies.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Les scientifiques ont découvert qu’une ancienne collision entre deux galaxies proches explique les mouvements inhabituels des étoiles du Petit Nuage de Magellan et apporte de nouveaux indices sur la matière noire.
articles.personality_title_es: Astrónomos revelan que el comportamiento extraño de una galaxia fue causado por una colisión cósmica
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – La Pequeña Nube de Magallanes (PNM) es una galaxia pequeña cerca de nuestra Vía Láctea. Durante años, los científicos notaron que sus estrellas se movían de manera confusa, diferente a la mayoría de galaxias donde las estrellas giran ordenadamente alrededor del centro. Este movimiento extraño desconcertaba a los astrónomos.

**Qué pasó** – Un nuevo estudio encontró que la PNM chocó con su vecina más grande, la Gran Nube de Magallanes (GNM), hace varios cientos de millones de años. Este choque cósmico sacudió las estrellas y el gas de la PNM, haciendo que sus movimientos parecieran caóticos en lugar de ordenados. La colisión incluso creó la ilusión de que el gas giraba cuando en realidad no era así.

**Impacto** – Este descubrimiento cambia cómo los científicos ven a la PNM. Antes se pensaba que era un ejemplo normal de galaxia pequeña, pero ahora está claro que la galaxia aún se está recuperando de un gran choque. Esto significa que la PNM podría no ser un buen modelo para entender cómo se forman y cambian otras galaxias. La investigación también ofrece una nueva forma de estudiar la materia oscura, una sustancia misteriosa en el espacio, observando cómo el choque inclinó partes de la galaxia más grande.

**Próximo paso** – Los investigadores usarán estos hallazgos para mejorar sus modelos sobre la formación y evolución de galaxias. También estudiarán cómo las colisiones afectan la materia oscura, ayudando a aprender más sobre este material invisible que da forma a las galaxias.

**Resumen en una frase** – Los científicos descubrieron que una colisión pasada entre dos galaxias cercanas explica los movimientos inusuales de las estrellas de la Pequeña Nube de Magallanes y ofrece nuevas pistas sobre la materia oscura.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm-toned painting of two intertwined, softly glowing galaxies—one smaller and irregularly shaped with scattered star-like dots, the other larger with a faint tilted bar structure—set against a dark cosmic backdrop, symbolizing a recent gentle cosmic collision that disrupts orderly motion yet reveals transformation and dynamic interaction.

articles.title: Private company to land on asteroid Apophis as it flies close to Earth
articles.summary: Apophis will be visited by multiple spacecraft – including landers – when it skims past Earth in three years
articles.content:
Apophis will be visited by multiple spacecraft – including landers – when it skims past Earth in three years

An artist’s impression of an asteroid flying near Earth Erik Simonsen/Getty Images

Two landers from a private US company will be part of an armada to asteroid Apophis when it flies past Earth in 2029.

Apophis, about 400 metres across, was discovered in 2004. Initial calculations showed it had an alarmingly high chance of hitting Earth – up to 2.7 per cent – in April 2029, in which case it could destroy an area the size of a city. Later refinements showed there was no chance of impact for at least 100 years.

Nevertheless, on 13 April 2029, the asteroid will pass extremely close to Earth, just 32,000 kilometres away, closer than geostationary satellites and near enough that it will be visible to the naked eye, a once-in-thousands-of-years event for an asteroid of this size. Multiple spacecraft from the US, Europe, Japan and China are planning to study the asteroid before, during and after the flyby.

Among those missions, the US company ExLabs has announced that its mothership spacecraft, called ApophisExL, passed a key review phase ahead of a planned launch in 2028. It will carry up to 10 spacecraft and instruments from different customers, including two landers, one from an unnamed source, and another from Japan’s Chiba Institute of Technology.

“The goal is to gain images from the surface of the asteroid,” says Miguel Pascual, the chief science officer and co-founder of ExLabs. “There’s some really exciting science that can happen.”

No private company has ever landed on an asteroid, although US asteroid mining firm Astroforge plans to launch a mission this year to land on an asteroid.

Free newsletter Sign up to Launchpad Bring the galaxy to your inbox every month, with the latest space news, launches and astronomical occurrences from New Scientist’s Leah Crane. Sign up to newsletter

ExLabs will deploy the Chiba Institute of Technology’s lander, which is the size of a shoebox, from 400 metres above Apophis. It will then descend at about 10 centimetres a second, gently touching down on the surface after an hour, with a camera taking images.

The landing will take place up to a week after Apophis’s flyby of Earth, to prevent any chance of accidentally changing the asteroid’s trajectory. Any collision in the lead-up to the flyby would be magnified by Earth’s gravity, says Pascual.

The European-Japanese mission to Apophis, called Ramses (Rapid Apophis Mission for Space Safety), will also include a lander, says Patrick Michel at Côte d’Azur University, the mission’s project scientist. It will touch down a few days before the flyby and will use a seismometer to measure any landslides caused by Earth’s gravitational tug – and could even record the touchdowns of ExLabs’ landers.

“Any opportunity to touch and feel the softness or hardness of the surface is great,” says Michel.

However, Michel urges effective communication between all the missions to ensure they run smoothly and don’t run into each other. “It is important that we coordinate,” he says. “The world will be watching. We don’t want to screw up.”
articles.reasoning: The article describes a significant upcoming space exploration event involving multiple spacecraft, including private company landers, visiting asteroid Apophis during its close Earth flyby in 2029. This mission represents a major scientific and technological milestone with broad public interest and potential to advance understanding of asteroids and planetary defense.
articles.personality_title: Private company to land spacecraft on asteroid Apophis during 2029 Earth flyby
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Apophis is a large asteroid, about 400 meters wide, that will pass very close to Earth in 2029. When it was discovered in 2004, there was worry it might hit Earth, but scientists later confirmed no impact will happen for at least 100 years. This close flyby is a rare chance to study such a big space rock up close.

**What happened** – A US private company called ExLabs is preparing to send a mothership spacecraft in 2028 that will carry up to 10 smaller spacecraft and instruments to Apophis. Among them are two landers, one from Japan’s Chiba Institute of Technology and another from an unnamed group. These landers will gently touch down on Apophis’s surface after it passes Earth to take pictures and gather data.

**Impact** – This mission is special because no private company has ever landed on an asteroid before. Studying Apophis closely will help scientists learn more about asteroids’ surfaces and how Earth’s gravity affects them during close flybys. It also shows how private companies are becoming important players in space exploration.

**What's next step** – The spacecraft will launch in 2028, arrive near Apophis around the 2029 flyby, and deploy the landers shortly after the asteroid passes Earth. Other missions from Europe, Japan, and China will also study Apophis, and all teams will coordinate to avoid problems during this busy event.

**One-sentence takeaway** – In 2029, private and international spacecraft will explore asteroid Apophis up close during its rare close flyby of Earth, marking a new step in asteroid science and space exploration.
articles.personality_title_fr: Une entreprise privée posera des engins sur l’astéroïde Apophis lors de son passage près de la Terre en 2029
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Apophis est un grand astéroïde d’environ 400 mètres de large qui passera très près de la Terre en 2029. Lors de sa découverte en 2004, on craignait qu’il ne percute la Terre, mais les scientifiques ont ensuite confirmé qu’aucun impact n’est prévu avant au moins 100 ans. Ce passage rapproché est une occasion rare d’étudier un si gros objet spatial de près.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Une entreprise privée américaine appelée ExLabs prépare l’envoi en 2028 d’un vaisseau-mère qui transportera jusqu’à 10 petits engins et instruments vers Apophis. Parmi eux, deux atterrisseurs, l’un de l’Institut de technologie de Chiba au Japon et un autre d’un groupe non nommé. Ces atterrisseurs toucheront doucement la surface d’Apophis après son passage près de la Terre pour prendre des photos et collecter des données.

**Impact** – Cette mission est unique car aucune entreprise privée n’a encore posé d’engin sur un astéroïde. Étudier Apophis de près aidera les scientifiques à mieux comprendre la surface des astéroïdes et comment la gravité terrestre les affecte lors de passages rapprochés. Cela montre aussi que les entreprises privées jouent un rôle important dans l’exploration spatiale.

**Prochaine étape** – Le lancement du vaisseau est prévu en 2028, avec arrivée près d’Apophis autour du passage en 2029. Les atterrisseurs seront déployés peu après le passage de l’astéroïde près de la Terre. D’autres missions européennes, japonaises et chinoises étudieront aussi Apophis, et toutes les équipes coordonneront leurs actions pour éviter tout problème.

**Résumé en une phrase** – En 2029, des engins spatiaux privés et internationaux exploreront de près l’astéroïde Apophis lors de son passage rare près de la Terre, marquant une avancée pour la science des astéroïdes et l’exploration spatiale.
articles.personality_title_es: Empresa privada aterrizará naves en el asteroide Apophis durante su paso cercano a la Tierra en 2029
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Apophis es un asteroide grande, de unos 400 metros de ancho, que pasará muy cerca de la Tierra en 2029. Cuando se descubrió en 2004, hubo preocupación de que pudiera chocar con la Tierra, pero luego los científicos confirmaron que no habrá impacto al menos por 100 años. Este paso cercano es una oportunidad rara para estudiar un objeto espacial tan grande de cerca.

**Qué pasó** – Una empresa privada de Estados Unidos llamada ExLabs está preparando el envío en 2028 de una nave nodriza que llevará hasta 10 pequeñas naves e instrumentos a Apophis. Entre ellas hay dos módulos de aterrizaje, uno del Instituto de Tecnología de Chiba en Japón y otro de un grupo no identificado. Estos módulos tocarán suavemente la superficie de Apophis después de que pase cerca de la Tierra para tomar fotos y obtener datos.

**Impacto** – Esta misión es especial porque ninguna empresa privada ha aterrizado antes en un asteroide. Estudiar Apophis de cerca ayudará a los científicos a aprender más sobre las superficies de los asteroides y cómo la gravedad de la Tierra los afecta durante acercamientos. También muestra cómo las empresas privadas están ganando importancia en la exploración espacial.

**Próximo paso** – La nave se lanzará en 2028, llegará cerca de Apophis alrededor del paso en 2029, y desplegará los módulos de aterrizaje poco después de que el asteroide pase por la Tierra. Otras misiones de Europa, Japón y China también estudiarán Apophis, y todos los equipos coordinarán sus actividades para evitar problemas.

**Frase clave** – En 2029, naves privadas e internacionales explorarán de cerca el asteroide Apophis durante su raro paso cercano a la Tierra, marcando un nuevo avance en la ciencia de asteroides y la exploración espacial.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm-toned painting of a large, rocky asteroid drifting closely past a softly glowing Earth, surrounded by a fleet of small, sleek spacecraft and delicate landers gently descending toward its surface, all rendered with natural, muted colors and subtle light emphasizing the calm, historic moment of exploration and peaceful cosmic encounter.

articles.title: We’ve spotted a huge asteroid spinning impossibly fast
articles.summary: Astronomers have found a 710-metre-wide asteroid that spins once every 1.9 minutes, so fast that it should have spun itself apart
articles.content:
Astronomers have found a 710-metre-wide asteroid that spins once every 1.9 minutes, so fast that it should have spun itself apart

Artist’s depiction of the asteroid 2025 MN45 NSF–DOE Vera C. Rubin Observatory/NOIRLab/SLAC​/AURA/P. Marenfeld

The Vera C. Rubin Observatory in Chile has spotted the fastest-rotating large asteroid ever seen. Despite measuring more than half a kilometre across, this asteroid spins about once every 1.9 minutes – a speed once thought to be impossible.

Dmitrii Vavilov at the University of Washington in Seattle and his colleagues found this asteroid, along with several other surprisingly speedy rotators, in the data from Rubin’s first nine nights of observations in late April and early May 2025. Vavilov presented the results at the Lunar and Planetary Science Conference in Texas on 17 March.

In that observation period, the researchers identified 76 asteroids for which they could reliably calculate rotational periods, with 19 of those being so-called super-fast rotators, spinning once every 2.2 hours or faster. That figure is the limit of how fast a “rubble pile” asteroid, made up of many smaller rocks loosely held together by gravity, can spin without falling apart.

The vast majority of asteroids are thought to be rubble piles, so the researchers didn’t expect to find many rotating faster than once every 2.2 hours. The fastest of the super-fast rotators spins once every 13 minutes or so. In their first set of analyses, the researchers didn’t even look for anything with a spin period of less than about 5 minutes, Vavilov said during his presentation. “We thought that was crazy that they could rotate any faster,” he said.

When they went back and looked for even faster rotators, they found three spinning so rapidly that they are considered ultra-fast rotators, with periods of about 3.8 minutes, 1.92 minutes and 1.88 minutes, respectively. The fastest, called 2025 MN45, has a diameter of about 710 metres and spins faster than any asteroid more than 500m across ever seen before.

Free newsletter Sign up to Launchpad Bring the galaxy to your inbox every month, with the latest space news, launches and astronomical occurrences from New Scientist’s Leah Crane. Sign up to newsletter

Its astonishing speed means this asteroid can’t possibly be a rubble pile. It must be made of much stronger mettle than most space rocks. “2.2 hours is supposed to be the limit for this asteroid, and yet it’s rotating in less than 2 minutes,” said Vavilov. “Even clay would not be enough to hold this asteroid together, so it’s probably one big rock or even solid metal.”

The Vera C. Rubin Observatory is expected to spot many more rotating asteroids over the course of its planned 10-year survey of the southern sky, enabling astronomers to explore the surprising diversity of these strange boulders in space.
articles.reasoning: The discovery of a large asteroid spinning at an unprecedented speed challenges existing scientific understanding of asteroid composition and structure, representing a significant breakthrough in space science with broad implications for planetary science and asteroid studies.
articles.personality_title: Astronomers discover giant asteroid spinning faster than ever seen
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Astronomers study asteroids to understand their size, shape, and how fast they spin. Most big asteroids are made of many small rocks loosely held together, called rubble piles, and they can only spin so fast before breaking apart.

**What happened** – Using the Vera C. Rubin Observatory in Chile, scientists found a huge asteroid named 2025 MN45 that is about 710 meters wide. This asteroid spins once every 1.9 minutes, much faster than what was thought possible for such a large space rock. The discovery was made during the observatory’s first nine nights of work in 2025.

**Impact** – This asteroid’s speed is surprising because if it were a rubble pile, it would have broken apart by spinning so fast. Instead, it must be made of solid rock or metal, which is much stronger. This challenges what scientists believed about how asteroids are built and shows that some can be much tougher than expected.

**What’s next step** – The Vera C. Rubin Observatory will continue its 10-year survey and likely find many more fast-spinning asteroids. This will help scientists learn more about the different types of asteroids and what they are made of.

**One-sentence takeaway** – A giant asteroid spinning faster than ever seen proves some space rocks are solid and stronger than scientists thought possible.
articles.personality_title_fr: Des astronomes découvrent un énorme astéroïde tournant plus vite que jamais
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les astronomes étudient les astéroïdes pour comprendre leur taille, leur forme et leur vitesse de rotation. La plupart des gros astéroïdes sont des amas de petits rochers appelés « tas de gravats », qui ne peuvent pas tourner trop vite sans se briser.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Grâce à l’observatoire Vera C. Rubin au Chili, des scientifiques ont découvert un énorme astéroïde nommé 2025 MN45, d’environ 710 mètres de large. Cet astéroïde tourne sur lui-même toutes les 1,9 minutes, beaucoup plus vite que ce que l’on croyait possible pour un objet aussi grand. Cette découverte a été faite lors des neuf premières nuits d’observations en 2025.

**Impact** – La vitesse de cet astéroïde est surprenante car s’il était un tas de gravats, il serait déjà tombé en morceaux à cause de cette rotation rapide. Il doit donc être fait de roche solide ou de métal, beaucoup plus résistant. Cela remet en question ce que les scientifiques pensaient sur la composition des astéroïdes.

**Prochaine étape** – L’observatoire Vera C. Rubin va continuer son étude sur 10 ans et devrait découvrir beaucoup d’autres astéroïdes qui tournent très vite. Cela aidera les scientifiques à mieux comprendre les différents types d’astéroïdes et leur composition.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Un énorme astéroïde tournant plus vite que jamais montre que certains corps spatiaux sont solides et plus résistants que prévu.
articles.personality_title_es: Astrónomos descubren un enorme asteroide girando más rápido que nunca
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Los astrónomos estudian los asteroides para entender su tamaño, forma y velocidad de giro. La mayoría de los asteroides grandes son pilas de rocas sueltas llamadas «montones de escombros» y no pueden girar demasiado rápido sin romperse.

**Qué pasó** – Usando el Observatorio Vera C. Rubin en Chile, los científicos encontraron un gran asteroide llamado 2025 MN45 que mide unos 710 metros de ancho. Este asteroide gira sobre sí mismo cada 1.9 minutos, mucho más rápido de lo que se creía posible para un objeto tan grande. El hallazgo ocurrió durante las primeras nueve noches de observación en 2025.

**Impacto** – La velocidad de este asteroide es sorprendente porque si fuera un montón de escombros, ya se habría roto por girar tan rápido. Por eso, debe estar hecho de roca sólida o metal, que es mucho más fuerte. Esto desafía lo que los científicos pensaban sobre la composición de los asteroides.

**Próximo paso** – El Observatorio Vera C. Rubin seguirá con su estudio de 10 años y probablemente encontrará muchos más asteroides que giran rápido. Esto ayudará a los científicos a aprender más sobre los diferentes tipos de asteroides y de qué están hechos.

**Frase resumen** – Un gran asteroide girando más rápido que nunca demuestra que algunas rocas espaciales son sólidas y más fuertes de lo que se pensaba.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm-toned painting of a large, rugged asteroid spinning rapidly in space, its rocky surface textured to suggest solid metal strength, set against a softly glowing starry background with a distant observatory dome silhouette watching from a serene, earthy landscape below.
